theorem_statement:
Jordan derangement theorem.

Let a finite group \(G\) act transitively on a finite set \(\Omega\) of size \(|\Omega|=n\ge 2\). Then there exists an element \(g\in G\) that fixes no point of \(\Omega\); that is,
\[
g\omega \ne \omega
\qquad\text{for every } \omega\in\Omega.
\]
Such an element is called a derangement.
raw_proof_text:
We prove that every transitive action of a finite group on a finite set of size at least two has a derangement.

Let \(G\) be a finite group acting transitively on a finite set \(\Omega\), and assume
\[
|\Omega| = n \ge 2.
\]
For each element \(g\in G\), let
\[
\operatorname{Fix}(g) := \{\omega\in\Omega : g\omega = \omega\}
\]
denote the set of fixed points of \(g\).

The key tool is Burnside's orbit-counting lemma. It states that the number of orbits of the action is
\[
\frac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G} |\operatorname{Fix}(g)|.
\]
Because the action is transitive, there is exactly one orbit. Therefore
\[
1 = \frac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G} |\operatorname{Fix}(g)|,
\]
and hence
\[
\sum_{g\in G} |\operatorname{Fix}(g)| = |G|.
\]

Now separate the contribution of the identity element \(e\in G\) from the contributions of the nonidentity elements. Since the identity fixes every point of \(\Omega\), we have
\[
|\operatorname{Fix}(e)| = |\Omega| = n.
\]
Thus
\[
|G| = n + \sum_{g\in G,\,g\ne e} |\operatorname{Fix}(g)|.
\]
Rearranging gives
\[
\sum_{g\in G,\,g\ne e} |\operatorname{Fix}(g)| = |G| - n.
\]

Suppose, for contradiction, that there is no derangement in \(G\). Then every nonidentity element fixes at least one point of \(\Omega\). In other words,
\[
|\operatorname{Fix}(g)| \ge 1
\qquad\text{for every } g\in G,\ g\ne e.
\]
There are exactly \(|G|-1\) nonidentity elements, so summing these inequalities yields
\[
\sum_{g\in G,\,g\ne e} |\operatorname{Fix}(g)| \ge |G|-1.
\]
But we already proved that the left-hand side equals \(|G|-n\). Therefore
\[
|G|-n \ge |G|-1.
\]
Subtracting \(|G|\) from both sides gives
\[
-n \ge -1,
\]
so
\[
n \le 1.
\]
This contradicts the hypothesis that \(n\ge 2\).

Therefore our assumption was false. Hence there exists some element \(g\in G\) such that
\[
|\operatorname{Fix}(g)| = 0.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
g\omega \ne \omega
\qquad\text{for every } \omega\in\Omega.
\]
So \(g\) is a derangement.

This completes the proof.
